commonMistakes: Mutating props or state instead of producing the next value.
Adding state or an Effect for a value that can be derived during render.
Using global state before identifying the real owner and consumers.
Optimizing renders without a measured user-facing problem.

contentAuthority.canonical: New JS2Next Release 1 curriculum and independently authored content.
contentAuthority.courseTranscripts: Coverage and sequencing research only; never learner-facing authority.
contentAuthority.legacyProjectContent: Supplementary internal reference only; excluded from runtime unless explicitly re-authored and approved.

choices.feedback: Engineering judgment balances clarity and cost. The concept should serve the requirement rather than become an end in itself.

researchSources.title: Watch Before You Start!
researchSources.usage: Coverage research only; no transcript wording is canonical.

sections.body: Follow the React pathway after the JavaScript prerequisites are comfortable. Treat each topic as a small cycle: predict, read, answer, explain, and review. Framework knowledge changes quickly, so separate durable mental models from version-specific APIs.
sections.title: How to use the React pathway

sections.body: React assumes comfort with JavaScript values, functions, arrays, objects, modules, and asynchronous code. Missing prerequisites are review signals, not barriers; revisit the smallest required concept and return.
sections.title: Expected prerequisites

summary: Course Orientation connects How to use the React pathway, Expected prerequisites.
Use the topic to make behavior and decisions explicit rather than memorizing isolated syntax.
Verify understanding by explaining a new example and answering the topic quiz without looking back.
title: Course Orientation

topicQuiz.failurePolicy: Show explanations, add missed objectives to local review, and allow immediate or later retry.

whyThisMatters: This orientation establishes how to use the React pathway deliberately. It explains the order of study, retrieval practice, and how mistakes become evidence for review rather than failure.
